title: Listening - Part 4: Environmental Activism
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five individuals discussing their involvement in local environmental projects

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d always been a passive supporter of green causes, but [21]the sight of plastic waste choking the local river where I walk my dog finally pushed me to take action[/21]. I organized a weekend cleanup. It was exhausting work, but [26]the most rewarding part was the sense of pride I felt when I saw the water clear for the first time in years[/26]. It’s made me much more vocal about environmental issues in my neighborhood. || Speaker 2: I’m a retired teacher, and [22]I joined a community gardening project because I wanted to share my knowledge of botany with the younger generation[/22]. I wanted to leave a legacy. It’s been a joy to see the kids' faces when they harvest their first carrots. However, [27]I found the constant need to fundraise for basic tools and seeds to be incredibly draining[/27]. It feels like we spend more time filling out grant applications than actually digging in the dirt. || Speaker 3: I work in marketing, and [23]I volunteered to help a local rewilding group because I felt my professional skills could help them reach a wider audience[/23]. I wanted to give something back. [28]What I didn't expect was the profound sense of connection to the seasons I developed through working outdoors[/28]. I used to barely notice the weather, but now I’m acutely aware of every bud and bird song. It’s been a wonderful boost for my mental health. || Speaker 4: I’ve always lived in the city, so [24]I started a rooftop beehive project primarily because I was worried about the declining bee population and wanted to do my bit[/24]. It’s a steep learning curve. [29]The biggest challenge has been dealing with the initial fear and complaints from some of my neighbors[/29]. I’ve had to spend a lot of time educating people about how gentle bees actually are. It’s taught me a lot about patience and effective communication. || Speaker 5: I’m a student, and [25]I joined a protest group against a new local highway because I couldn't stand the thought of losing our last remaining ancient woodland[/25]. It was a very intense experience. [30]The most significant outcome for me has been the amazing network of like-minded friends I’ve built[/30]. I used to feel quite isolated in my beliefs, but now I feel part of a powerful community. It’s given me a much more optimistic outlook on the future of our planet.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what motivated each speaker to get involved.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 was moved to action by seeing plastic waste in her local river.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted to share her botany knowledge with the younger generation.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 felt her marketing skills could help the group reach a wider audience.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 was worried about the decline in bees and wanted to help.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted to prevent the destruction of ancient woodland by a new highway.

options.A: to meet a requirement for a course
options.B: to address a specific ecological threat
options.C: to pass on expertise to others
options.D: to gain a new professional qualification
options.E: to lower their own carbon footprint
options.F: to respond to a distressing local sight
options.G: to utilize their career background for good
options.H: to protect a specific natural area

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions about their experience.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions the pride of seeing the tangible results of a clean river.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 found the constant need for fundraising to be draining.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 developed a profound connection to the seasons and nature.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 struggled with the initial fear and complaints from her neighbors.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 built an amazing network of like-minded friends.

options.A: observing a visible change in the environment
options.B: a heightened awareness of natural cycles
options.C: the difficulty of finding land to use
options.D: an increase in their physical strength
options.E: a change in their personal shopping habits
options.F: the frustration of securing financial support
options.G: handling negative reactions from the public
options.H: the benefit of a new social support group
